question.text: Mr. Verma contributes Rs. 1,50,000 to NPS Tier I under Section 80CCD(1) and an additional Rs. 50,000 under Section 80CCD(1B). His employer also contributes 14% of his basic + DA (Rs. 70,000 per month) to NPS. What is his total NPS-related tax deduction?

answer.text: Rs. 3,17,600, but the employee's 80CCD(1) deduction is subject to the overall 80C ceiling of Rs. 1,50,000, so net additional benefit is 80CCD(1B) Rs. 50,000 + employer's 80CCD(2) Rs. 1,17,600
Rs. 2,67,600 (Rs. 1,50,000 under 80CCD(1) capped at Rs. 1,00,000 + Rs. 50,000 under 80CCD(1B) + employer's 14%)

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** NPS tax deductions work across three sections: (1) 80CCD(1): Employee contribution up to 10% of salary, but within the overall Rs. 1,50,000 limit of Section 80C/80CCC/80CCD(1). (2) 80CCD(1B): Additional Rs. 50,000 over and above 80C limit. (3) 80CCD(2): Employer contribution up to 14% of basic + DA (for government; 10% for others) — no cap, not part of 80C limit. Employer's contribution = 14% × Rs. 70,000 × 12 = Rs. 1,17,600. Total deductions possible: Rs. 1,50,000 (within 80C) + Rs. 50,000 (80CCD(1B)) + Rs. 1,17,600 (80CCD(2)) = Rs. 3,17,600.
**The Trap:** The first option ignores the employer's deduction entirely. The key nuance is that 80CCD(1) is NOT in addition to 80C — it's within the 80C umbrella. Only 80CCD(1B) and 80CCD(2) are truly additional deductions. Many candidates double-count by treating 80CCD(1) as separate from 80C.
**Cross-Exam Context:** NPS taxation is heavily tested across NISM X-A, X-B, and income tax certifications. The interplay between 80C, 80CCD(1), 80CCD(1B), and 80CCD(2) is one of the most complex tax topics in retirement planning.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always max out 80CCD(1B) first — it's the easiest Rs. 50,000 additional deduction. For government employees, the 14% employer deduction under 80CCD(2) is extremely valuable. Help clients understand that 80CCD(1) competes with other 80C instruments like PPF, ELSS, and insurance.
